Guide To Writing
Ridiculously Successfui Ernails

AI1 highly successful people are crazy and extreme. If
people don't think you're nuts you're simply not trying
hard enough. push the limits.
( https z / /twílu|uer. com/WaIlStPIayboys,/status/
67 7 L42808 42 428 4t62? s=0 9 )

learning about his market at that deep a level. I was pushing him to try some crazy "ortt

put their lists and market on a pedestal, and do all the things that prevent them from
cutting loose and getting a bit crazy in their emails. Most people are too scared to do
these things. They want to be "professional" and "nice" and "safe." But those things
aren't soins to make vou stick out in the inbox. You have to be a little crazv to win the	oo¿
email game. Obüously, you have to modulate the crazy depending on your market and
product. But there's a big diflerence between email writers who play to not lose and who
play to win. Those who play to not lose hold back, live in fear of what people will think,
and never make the sales they could and should make. On the other hand, people who
play to win go allJack Bauer in their emails. If you want to see someone who is drive to
accomplish his goals at any cost watch the show "24" . Jack Bauer is crazy. And he has to
be to get the job done - even if that means hijacking convenience stores and airplanes,
and all the other crazy stuff he did in that show. Like it or lump it, you have to embrace
your crazy and even love it a little if you want to be the best, most successful email writer
you can be.
IIow To flse The
ssMouth Breather" Subject Line
To Give Readers No Choice
But To Open Your Ernails!
Since we're talking about cÍazy, check this out: When I spoke at AWAI's Web
Copywriting Intensive event last February/March, I remember Clayton Makepeace
sayng his favorite subject line was one of the subject lines I have since dubbed "The
Mouth Breather" subject line. Basically, what that is, is a hlper creepy version of the
Shock Effect subject line I write about in the Email Plalters Plastbook. The idea is, to say
something so creepy and startling, they have to wonder if (1) it's a personal email from
you and (2) if you could be some kind of creeper and (3) if you really do it right... if you
are STALKI|{G them somehow. Oooooooh yeah, baby! The ideal way is if they (if even
for a split second) think "holy shnikes, is he/she watching me???" For example: When I
first moved to the Burgle back in Fall of 2013 (exactly two years before I moved back to
the coast, not that this has anything to do with anything) and first hired my ex-
copywriting apprentice to clean my house, she proceeded to stalk my Facebook page and,
yes, join my email list. The very FIRST email she got from me was at 3 am (I used to send

my emails out at 3 arn PST). And, it had the mouth breather subject line:
ttStop watching porn"
I\o*, I didn't send that out knowing she would read it. But, it had the exact aflect I
wanted it to have on her and anyone else reading it. She said she got a little creeped out.
Do you think that made an impact on her? Hmm? You're dang right it did. That was her
first exposure (heh) to my way of doing emails and she was hooked from then on. Another
example: the one Clayton Makepeace liked so much was:
ttl think about you in the shower"
The first time I used that email (and I've recycled it many times over the years, it's
one of my favorites forjolting my list a bit) I remember a few people getting especially
mad at me. One guy just replied back within seconds of it going out (he didn't even read
the email, just the subject line) saying "unsubscribe me!!!" You KNOW you're doing it
right when you get the mush cookies mushing like little bitches like that. Here's another
example:
Talk dirty to rne, [first narne]
Oh man, that one was hair raising. I did that email subject line back when I first
started mailing daily and got a bunch of guys angry at me (the chicls didn't seem to mind
so much... h-...)
Here's another one to warm the cockles of your lists's hearts:
"Why do you keep watching rny videos?"
Obüously this will work best if you do a lot of üdeo. You could also go with
something like, "Quit stalking rny Facebook profile". That'd be a fun one...
Ary*uy, notice how none of these subject lines are like anything you see anyone
else doing (well, there might be some people doing them, but they are few and far
between, Iet's face it). There is no benefit hinted at. I{othing smacking of "copyrvriting" to
them. They make no promises, imply no promises, or even create a thought of the usual
advertising claims or promises. The only purpose of them is to jolt your list, wake 'em up,

and get their undivided attention. This goes back to the Wall Street Playboy lesson above:

Hardcore Ernail Writing Tip
Frorn The Kirg Of Cool
One of my favorite actors was the late Steve McQree.r. With no real education or
formal training (just a desire to be the best and compete) he became the single highest
paid actor in the world of his day. And, there are many things he did to stay on top and
confound his competitors. Like, for example, when he was tqnrg to make a name for
himself he would "steal" scenes from bigger name actors by doing things like shaking his
hat or gun so even if it was another actor's shot. By doing this, he got noticed and
everyone thought he was the star of the movie "The Magnificent Seven". Another thing
he did (and this is where the email writing tip comes in) is, he was notorious for "saying"
pages of dialogue with a single look. In fact, I know a guy in the entertainment business
who sells stuff to aspiring actors and he was saying how it wasn't uncommon for Steve
McQueen to toss out 2-3 pages of dialogue while filming because he would stare at the
camera in such away where all that needed to be said was said with a look, and no
dialogue was necessary. This also applies to email and sales copy, too. By using idioms you
will find you can sometimes "ruy" in a sentence or two (or in just a few words) that is far
more than you can say with 3 paragraphs of drivel that takes forever to get to the point.
And you know what? I think it'd be helpful to you, as someone who wants to be not just
good but great at writing emails to have some figures of speeches standing by. That way,
you can dip into it anytime you want to say more with fewer words in your emails. I found
a particular site online I've been toying with myself, that I think will help any email writer
craft more interesting, more entertaining and far more clear emails.
-10-

A Testirnonial Ernail Ternplate
For Your Righteous Booty
I mentioned testimonial emails earlier but, I have noticed a lot of testimonial
emails I see are complicating it and, probably, holding back sales. Below is some adüce I
gave to Misty the Ben Settle Show announcer chick when she was writing emails to
promote my Copy Slacker launch to that list after I noticed one of her testimonial emails
was too long and wordy. Ilere is the template:
Subject line: (whatever you want to say
(name of person) uses (name of product)
( testimonial )
- use curiosity when in doubt)
to get (benefit):
Grab (name of product) here by (deadrine) to get it for (discount):
(LrNK)
Signature
Yes, it really is that simple and the more you can make the email above the fold,
the better. Of course, you can play around with the CTA and first sentence however you
want, and if it's a gold card testimonial, or a testimonial from someone with impressive
credentials, make sure you include those (i.e. "TomJones - a fitness trainer for multiple
celebrities, says..."). But my most profitable testimonial emails often use this exact
template. Often times I will spend a lot more time on the close in mine (after the
testimonial). But, it's not always necessary. And, in fact, it's perfectly okay to just go

elBenbo Ceases
His Hypocritical Ways
So last lr[ovember I took another consulting client on (a diflerent one from the one
I mentioned before). Ary*ry, one of the many pieces of advice I gave him during that
month was to claim the title of being the best at what he does. I don't remember how I
suggested he word it, but it was pretty arrogant and ballsy and I think it made him a bit
nervous. It's one thing to behave like you're the best at what you do. It's quite another to
put it in writing for the world to see. And, thus, he declined. But you know what? I
couldn't even blame him. Here I was telling him to call himself the leader in his industry
at what he does, but I had never actually done that for my own business. That is, until
earlier this year. I decided to take my own advice (which I originally heard from pubticity
I(I}[G Paul Hartunian who has been saying for years people should be claiming the title
of being the best). And, I started caliing myself on my squeeze pages, etc "The World
Leader In Email Copywriting Education." Ir[ow, whether that's technically true, who
knows? At a certain level who is "best" at an¡hing is always subjective. But, I think I can
definitely make a good case for myself in this regard considering my products, track
record, and long and distinguished list of testimonials (especia\ the caliber of those
testimonials). The result? I don't know I can direct trace it to the title. But I did notice
opts ins going up and sales going up. But agaín,whether it's from that, I knoweth not.
The reason I'm telling you this is, I now want YOU to do the same thing: Claim a title.
I\ot only do I believe it'll help with your sales, but this is probably the easiest ways to help
psiüon yourself in your marketplace. The better your positioning, the more money you
make. Even grocery stores know this (which is why food manufacturers fight tooth and
nail for eye level positioning on the shelves - it increases sales regardless of the quality or
taste or nutritional value of the food do to superior positioning). An1,way, to inspire you to
claim a title following are some people who have claimed titles and the titles they've
claimed. Pick a dramatic, catchy, fun and benefit-oriented title (whether you created it or
someone else calls you it) and it can do wonders for your market place positioning.
Here are some real life examples:

Of Hot Leads By Getting Booked
On Popular Podcasts
One of the single best traffic sources I get is podcast traffic. By that I mean,
someone interviews me on their podcast, I give my link at the end, and I get a steady flow
of hot leads. And by hot leads I mean, leads that have (1) listened to me yap and squawk
about whatever the topic is (usually email cop¡,vriting and marketing) for 30-60 minutes
and (2) then go to my site and opt in. That is a hot lead. They don't come in huge
abundance (not usually, at least). But, the ones I do get are of such a high quality and
caliber, that I would gladly have 100 podcast leads than 1,000 leads from practically
anywhere else. Something else to think about: Podcasting is basically the new talk radio.
If you ever heard publicity king Paul Hartunian talk about it - radio is probably the best
kind of publicity you can get. It's direct response friendly (billions of dollars of sales -most from direct response ads - are done üa am radio each year). And, it's easy to do
since you don't have to travel. So, very good way to get leads. The problem a lot of people
have is getting booked on shows, though. There are people who will charge you through
the nose to do this for you (one chick quoted me $15,000 for 3 months of help to get me
booked on shows). But, if you know the right people, it's free.
The two people who have got me booked on the most shows areJonthan Rivera
("Email Players" subscriber and the man who produces my own podcast) and a fellow by
the name of Luis Congdon. Luis has products that show you how to do it (I highly
recommend, btw). And, he's probably the most well-connected podcaster out there.
(Certainly one of them.) Anyway, a few months ago he posted the folloüng in the Street
Smart Bidniz group about how to get yourself booked on shows without spending $15k or
whatever. And, since he's helped me get a bunch of free publicity online, I figure I'11 do
the same for him.
Here goes:
Vüe all love emails. they help you seII.
But growing the email list has been the challenge for many. And gettinq

in front of the right people is also another challenge.
Here's one hray f've been making sales with a small 1ist, found tons of
prospects, and leveraged other lists and networks.
I've pitched myself to get on podcasts. I've gotten on 100+ shows. Of
every size. Ones that do thousands of downloads and others that do 100s
and others that have practically no listeners.
Each show has herped me hone my message, pitch without rearry pitching,
and garner more subscribers and buyers - some of the highest quality
ones that you can get.
Edison research has found that a podcast lj-steners tends to be in their
30s and 40s, is upper middle class and will spend 20-30 minutes
listening to a podcast epj-sode. That's pretty freaking a$resome for us
marketers. coaches, consultants, and sales guys.
Getting on shows can be laborious unless you have an ample network of
podcasters like r do. But |ve got a solid method you can use to help
you.
Go into iTunes. Find the new shows.
write them a pitch. But use my copy and paste method to herp make it a
tad bit faster.
1. Simple compliment. Look at show description. There's a simple
compliment you can give - this is faster than rj-stenj-ng to a whore
shor,¡.
2. Look at guests they've had.
3. After compriment use your copy n paste message that tarks about how
you serve audiences.
Now do this for 3-5 shows a day. and you'11 start getting responses.
Arthough r host a group of 800+ podcasters and have a podcast course
and on average do 10-30 shows a month r still use the method above.
This little method generates for me some of the highest quality reads
and crients, one of my crients by being on one show sol-d a $9k program
and another sold a $5k program (funny thing is both these sales were
done via some tiny tiny shows)
It's not the size of the show but the quality of listenership.
